serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of prawn omelette in a Singapore Chinese restaurant is approximately 200 g, consisting of a pan-fried egg patty studded with whole or chopped prawns.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, chicken, whole, fried (HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for prawn omelette)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single HPB Foodo entry exists for 虾煎蛋 (prawn omelette) as a composite dish. Values are derived by adapting HPB Singapore data for fried egg dishes combined with USDA FoodData Central data for cooked shrimp (USDA SR Legacy #15152) and cooking oil contribution. Macros reflect a typical restaurant-style preparation: approximately 70% egg base, 20% prawn, 10% oil and seasoning. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with comparable HPB entries for egg-based fried dishes (e.g., HPB 'Egg, fried' ~220 kcal/100g adjusted for prawn addition). This is an adapted/estimated composite; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg-based dishes and mixed protein-fat meals
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn omelette. GI estimated at 50 based on analogous egg-based fried dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Egg omelette and fried egg dishes are consistently low-GI foods (GI range 40–55) due to negligible carbohydrate content and high protein-fat matrix that slows gastric emptying. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (50 × 1.8 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2) / 100 = 1.8 g, confirming very low GL. GI category: low (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100g) from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and request reduced soy sauce, as the sodium content of approximately 320 mg per 100 g can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~1.8 g/100g) and low glycemic index (~50)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn omelette is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content of approximately 1.8 g per 100 g and low glycemic index of 50, resulting in a glycemic load of under 2 per serving; however, the high cholesterol content warrants monitoring in those with concurrent dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content (~310 mg/100g) from eggs and saturated fat from frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume prawn omelette in moderation given its high dietary cholesterol (~310 mg/100g) and saturated fat (~3.4 g/100g); choosing healthier cooking oils and limiting frequency to occasional consumption is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn omelette (虾煎蛋, Xia Jian Dan) is a classic Chinese and Singaporean egg dish made by pan-frying or wok-frying beaten eggs with whole or chopped prawns (shrimp) in oil, typically seasoned with soy sauce, salt, and spring onion. The result is a golden-brown, slightly crispy-edged omelette with a soft, custardy interior studded with tender, savoury prawns. It is a popular zi char (Chinese-style cook-to-order) dish in Singapore hawker centres and restaurants, often served as a shared side dish alongside rice. Nutritionally, prawn omelette is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with very low carbohydrate content (~1.8 g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. It provides approximately 185 kcal per 100 g, with 13.5 g protein and 13.2 g fat. The dish is rich in dietary cholesterol (~310 mg/100g) due to the egg yolks, and contains moderate sodium (~320 mg/100g) from seasoning. It is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, and vitamin A. The glycemic index is low (~50), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. Clinically, it is well-tolerated by people with Type 2 Diabetes but should be consumed in moderation by those with cardiovascular disease or hypertension due to its cholesterol and sodium content.
common_user_queries: Is prawn omelette good for diabetics?
Is prawn omelette high in cholesterol?
meal_context: lunch or dinner main protein dish

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists specifically for 虾煎蛋 (prawn omelette) as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB fried egg dish data and USDA cooked shrimp data using a defensible ingredient proportion model. GI is estimated from analogous egg-based dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values are composite estimates rather than direct measurements of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' as prawn omelette is an egg-and-seafood dish, not a grain or starch. 2. Nutrient values are adapted composite estimates; no single HPB or USDA entry covers this exact dish. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for fried eggs and cooked shrimp; these are not -1 sentinel values but estimated numeric values. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record. 3. GI set to 50 as an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database egg-based dish data; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 4. Cuisine tag 'Noodle' in input is not applicable to this dish and has been disregarded. 5. Price data (SGD 22.00) from Bei Jiang Restaurant 北疆饭店 is noted but not used as a nutritional source.
